retrieved_docs.title: Over-The-Counter Drugs Laws -- Issues of Concern -- Food and Drug Administration Safety and Innovation Act (2012)
retrieved_docs.content: In 2002, a Federal Court ruled that the Pediatric Rule was invalid. Following this ruling in 2002, the FDAMA was reintroduced as the Best Pharmaceutical for Children Act, which was signed into law in 2002. In addition to the patent extension, companies were granted government funding to complete the pediatric studies.[ NIH. BPCA ] In 2003, the Pediatric Research Equity Act, which contained components of the Pediatric Rule, was introduced. The PREA allows the FDA to require pediatric studies to determine safety and efficacy in children, appropriate dosing, and special formulations (ie, liquid) for children. In addition, a company producing a medication without pediatric testing must submit a waiver to the FDA explaining why the testing can not reasonably or safely be accomplished. While the BPCA and the PREA were reauthorized, they needed to be renewed every 5 years. In 2012, when the Food and Drug Administration Safety and Innovation Act (FDASIA) was signed into law, Title V section 507 permanently reauthorized the BPCA and PREA without requiring a 5-year renewal (FDA. FDASIA).

retrieved_docs.title: Federal Regulation of Medication Dispensing -- Issues of Concern -- Controlled Substances Act of 1971
retrieved_docs.content: Most cold medications, cough preparations, and antidiarrheals fall into Schedule V if they contain codeine. With these schedules in place, the FDA and Drug Enforcement Agency (DEA) are able to monitor the sale, distribution, and dispensing of all controlled substances in the United States [21 U.S.C.§ 812].

retrieved_docs.title: Upper Respiratory Tract Infections With Focus on The Common Cold -- Toxicity and Adverse Effect Management
retrieved_docs.content: In children younger than 6 years, significant risks are associated with the administration of over-the-counter and prescription cough and cold medications. Most notably, overdose due to antihistamines and decongestants can be life-threatening. Similarly, the use of codeine should be avoided in the pediatric population. Inappropriate use of antibiotics is a significant contributor to the emergence of antibiotic resistance, and they can cause significant adverse reactions.

retrieved_docs.title: Upper Respiratory Tract Infections With Focus on The Common Cold -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The rhinovirus has no effective antiviral treatments. Therefore, therapy for the common cold is directed toward symptom relief. While hundreds of over-the-counter and behind-the-counter common cold “remedies” contain various ingredients, very few have undergone large randomized clinical trials, and even fewer have been extensively tested in children. Over-the-counter common cold medications should not be administered to children younger than 4 years due to safety concerns and lack of efficacy. In both adults and children, the use of common cold medications requires caution to prevent potential overdose, toxicity, and drug interactions. [26] [27] [CDC Common Cold Treatment]

retrieved_docs.title: Over-The-Counter Drugs Laws -- Issues of Concern -- Food and Drug Administration Safety and Innovation Act (2012)
retrieved_docs.content: This act made the Best Pharmaceutical for Children Act (BPCA) and the Pediatric Research Equity Act (PREA) permanent, however, they do not apply to all legend drugs. The BPCA and the PREA were established to ensure companies consider the pediatric population when seeking approval for new medications. In 1997, the Food and Drug Administration Modernization Act (FDAMA) introduced a voluntary incentive program to encourage pediatric studies when testing medications for safety and efficacy. The incentive included an additional 6 months for the medication's patent for market exclusivity. When companies were not taking advantage of the voluntary incentive, possibly due to the added cost of additional studies, it was replaced by the Pediatric Rule in 1998. The Pediatric Rule made pediatric studies mandatory for any drug product likely to be used in children, even if the company was not seeking approval for pediatric use.

retrieved_docs.content: Accidently toxic ingestion of acetaminophen by children can be reduced with safe storage practices out of reach from children and utilizing packaging that utilize childproof safety precautions. Also, in adults, unintentional toxic ingestion can be reduced with education about the many non-prescription products which contain acetaminophen.

Honey has antitussive properties but should not be used in children younger than 1 year of age due to the risk of botulism. retrieved_docs.title: Federal Regulation of Medication Dispensing -- Issues of Concern -- Poison Prevention and Packaging Act of 1970
retrieved_docs.content: The main provision that has been instituted since the passage of the PPPA is the use of safety caps on all prescriptions. Under the PPPA, all new prescriptions must have a child-resistant cap unless the prescriber or the patient specifies one is unnecessary. A provider may request that a prescription be filled without a child-resistant cap, but it is only for specific medications and subsequent refills. Ultimately, the patient decides whether a child-resistant cap is placed on their prescription. Additionally, there are a few medications that are exempt from the requirements of the PPPA. Some examples include sublingual nitroglycerin, chewable aspirin, oral contraceptives, and aerosol inhalation products [16 CFR § 1700.14].

retrieved_docs.title: Federal Regulation of Medication Dispensing -- Issues of Concern -- Poison Prevention and Packaging Act of 1970
retrieved_docs.content: The Poison Prevention and Packaging Act of 1970 (PPPA) was instituted by the federal government to protect children under the age of 5 years from accidental poisoning. This Act enforces labeling requirements and child-safe packaging [16 CFR § 1700.15]. Although this Act is enforced on the federal level, the Consumer Products Safety Commission (CPSC) monitors compliance rather than the FDA. The Commission ensures that all medications be placed in specific packaging to curb the incidence of accidental child poisoning [16 CFR § 1700.2]. Safety labeling must be placed on all packaging, whether or not the prescription is secured with a child-resistant cap.

retrieved_docs.title: Federal Regulation of Medication Dispensing -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Finally, the FDA instituted the Poison Prevention and Packaging Act of 1970 (PPPA) to protect the public and particularly children. This law established the required safety precautions that must be taken when dispensing medications to protect children under the age of 5 years from accidental poisoning [16 CFR § 1700].

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2483
retrieved_docs.content: Aliskiren, a direct renin inhibitor, is contraindicated for use in preschool children (předškolní dítě), indicating that this medication should not be administered to patients of that age group. In addition to its clinical contraindication, aliskiren is chemically classified as an amide (amidy), and its molecular structure has been derived from a reference source (mapped_from). These points underscore both the importance of age‑specific safety considerations and the relevance of understanding the drug’s chemical classification when prescribing aliskiren.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1499
retrieved_docs.content: Aliskiren and bisoprolol are antihypertensive agents that share a common contraindication in patients with urinary retention (انقطاع البول). In addition, aliskiren is specifically contraindicated in preschool children (předškolní dítě), reflecting age‑related safety concerns for this renin‑angiotensin system inhibitor. Recognizing these contraindications is essential for clinicians to avoid prescribing aliskiren to young children and to avoid both aliskiren and bisoprolol in individuals who experience urinary retention, thereby optimizing patient safety and therapeutic outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2850
retrieved_docs.content: Pediatric Adverse Events Terminology incorporates the concept وفاة حديث الولادة, which denotes neonatal death, as a specific entry within its classification of pediatric adverse events. By including neonatal death in this terminology, healthcare providers can standardize reporting, track incidence, and implement targeted strategies to improve safety and outcomes for newborn patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2523
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature). Short stature, in turn, is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death). وفاة حديث الولادة is identified as a concept within the Pediatric Adverse Events Terminology, indicating that neonatal death is recognized as a specific adverse event in pediatric care. Together, these relationships suggest that infants born preterm with low birth weight may develop short stature, which is linked to an increased risk of neonatal death, a critical adverse outcome captured by pediatric safety terminology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2861
retrieved_docs.content: The Pediatric Adverse Events Terminology encompasses a range of specific adverse‑event concepts relevant to children, and it includes the concept تدلّي الحبل السري (umbilical cord prolapse). By incorporating this particular adverse event, the terminology provides clinicians and researchers with a standardized term for identifying, reporting, and managing umbilical cord prolapse in pediatric patients, thereby supporting consistent communication and improved patient safety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 775
retrieved_docs.content: Cilostazol, aliskiren, and methyldopa each exhibit distinct pharmacologic and chemical characteristics that are important for patient safety. Cilostazol is chemically classified as a 1H‑tetrazole derivative, an amide, and an azole, indicating that its molecular structure contains a tetrazole ring, an amide functional group, and an azole heterocycle. Aliskiren also belongs to the 1H‑tetrazole class and contains an azole moiety, and it is linked to amide‑related chemistry through a mapped relationship. Both cilostazol and aliskiren share a common safety concern: they are contraindicated in individuals with hypersensitivity reactions (فرط تحسس). In addition, aliskiren carries a specific pediatric restriction, being contraindicated in preschool‑aged children (předškolní dítě). Methyldopa is identified as a centrally acting antiadrenergic agent, placing it within the class of drugs that reduce sympathetic outflow from the central nervous system. Like the other two agents, methyldopa is also contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity. Recognizing these chemical classifications and contraindications helps clinicians select appropriate therapies, avoid adverse drug reactions, and tailor treatment plans to individual patient risk factors.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1788
retrieved_docs.content: Aspirin is listed as a concept within both the NCIt COVID‑19 Agent Terminology and the broader NCIt COVID‑19 Terminology, indicating that it is recognized as a relevant agent in the context of COVID‑19 research and classification. In the same terminologies, the vitamin D metabolite kalcifediol is also included, reflecting its consideration as a potential therapeutic or investigational substance for COVID‑19. Additionally, the mineral supplement síran zinečnatý (zinc sulfate) appears as a concept in both the NCIt COVID‑19 Agent Terminology and the NCIt COVID‑19 Terminology, underscoring its relevance to COVID‑19–related discussions, likely due to its immunomodulatory properties. Aspirin carries a specific safety warning: it is contraindicated in předškolní dítě (preschool‑aged children), a precaution that aligns with established concerns about aspirin use in young children, such as the risk of Reye syndrome. Together, these relationships illustrate how various pharmacologic agents—including aspirin, kalcifediol, and zinc sulfate—are organized within standardized COVID‑19 vocabularies while also highlighting important age‑related contraindications for aspirin use in pediatric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1519
retrieved_docs.content: Pediatric Adverse Events Terminology encompasses the concept تسرع القلب (tachycardia) and also includes the concept وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death) as adverse events relevant to children. INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature), indicating that infants born preterm with low birth weight are at increased risk of developing reduced height. Additionally, قصر القامة (short stature) is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), suggesting that short stature may be linked to a higher likelihood of death in the newborn period. Together, these relationships highlight a clinical pathway in which preterm low‑birth‑weight infants may experience short stature, which in turn is associated with neonatal mortality, and both tachycardia and neonatal death are recognized adverse events within the Pediatric Adverse Events Terminology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2759
retrieved_docs.content: Pediatric Adverse Events Terminology is a structured classification that incorporates specific pediatric safety concepts, including the adverse event “تدلّي الحبل السري” (umbilical cord prolapse) and the outcome “وفاة حديث الولادة” (neonatal death). By grouping these concepts within subsets of the terminology, the system facilitates systematic identification, reporting, and management of critical pediatric adverse events, thereby supporting improved patient safety and clinical care for newborns.
